title: Optional Advanced: Likelihoods with optim
text:
Pooled mosquito surveillance
Suppose a health department monitors West Nile virus in mosquito pools.

20 pools were tested.
Each pool contains 10 mosquitoes.
7 pools were positive.

If the individual mosquito infection risk is p, the probability a pool tests positive is:
\[
P(+) = 1 - (1 - p)^{10}
\]

title: Optional Advanced: Likelihoods with optim
text:
Write the likelihood
For 7 positive pools out of 20:
\[
Y \sim Binomial(20, 1 - (1 - p)^{10})
\]
In R, we can write the negative log likelihood directly.

pooled_nll <- function(p) {
  pool_positive_probability <- 1 - (1 - p)^10

  -dbinom(
    x = 7,
    size = 20,
    prob = pool_positive_probability,
    log = TRUE
  )
}

title: Optional Advanced: Likelihoods with optim
text:
Estimate the infection risk

pooled_fit <- optim(
  par = 0.05,
  fn = pooled_nll,
  method = "L-BFGS-B",
  lower = 0.00001,
  upper = 0.99999,
  hessian = TRUE
)

pooled_fit

$par
[1] 0.04217128

$value
[1] 1.690642

$counts
function gradient 
       8        8 

$convergence
[1] 0

$message
[1] "CONVERGENCE: REL_REDUCTION_OF_F <= FACTR*EPSMCH"

$hessian
         [,1]
[1,] 4051.487



infection_risk <- pooled_fit$par
infection_risk

[1] 0.04217128

title: Optional Advanced: Likelihoods with optim
text:
Approximate confidence interval
For a one-parameter likelihood, a quick Wald-style interval uses the Hessian.

pooled_se <- sqrt(1 / pooled_fit$hessian[1, 1])
pooled_ci <- infection_risk + c(-1.96, 1.96) * pooled_se

c(
  estimate = infection_risk,
  lower = pooled_ci[1],
  upper = pooled_ci[2]
)

  estimate      lower      upper 
0.04217128 0.01137851 0.07296406 


This approximation is convenient, but it is not magic. Check whether the parameter is near a boundary before trusting it.

title: Optional Advanced: Likelihoods with optim
text:
Practice checkpoint: zero inflation
Prompt
Some trap regions may have no West Nile virus introduced yet. Let kappa be the probability that the region is exposed to the risk process at all.
Use:
\[
P(+) = \kappa \left(1 - (1 - p)^{10}\right)
\]
Estimate p and kappa from the same 7 positive pools out of 20.

title: Optional Advanced: Likelihoods with optim
text:
Practice checkpoint: hint
Your function should take a length-two vector:
zip_nll <- function(par) {
  p <- par[1]
  kappa <- par[2]

  ...
}
Use length-two par, lower, and upper values in optim().

title: Optional Advanced: Likelihoods with optim
text:
Practice checkpoint: solution

zip_nll <- function(par) {
  p <- par[1]
  kappa <- par[2]

  pool_positive_probability <- kappa * (1 - (1 - p)^10)

  -dbinom(
    x = 7,
    size = 20,
    prob = pool_positive_probability,
    log = TRUE
  )
}

zip_fit <- optim(
  par = c(p = 0.05, kappa = 0.70),
  fn = zip_nll,
  method = "L-BFGS-B",
  lower = c(0.00001, 0.00001),
  upper = c(0.99999, 0.99999)
)

zip_fit$par

         p      kappa 
0.06629598 0.70510949 


With only one aggregate count, many combinations of p and kappa can produce similar fits. The model is easy to code, but the data may not identify both parameters well.

title: Optional Advanced: Fitting ODE Models
text:
Practice checkpoint
Prompt
Change the starting values in optim() to beta = 0.005 and gamma = 0.50. Do the fitted parameters return to approximately the same values?
Hint
Only change the par = ... line. Keep the same bounds.
Solution outline
Run the same call with:
par = c(beta = 0.005, gamma = 0.50)
For this small example, the bounds and data are informative enough that the result should be very similar. For more complex ODE systems, starting values can matter a lot.

title: Optional Advanced: Fitting ODE Models
text:
Take-home cautions

Real case data are noisy, delayed, underreported, and often only partly observed.
The observation model matters as much as the ODE model.
Parameter uncertainty from this quick example is not reliable.
Use this deck to learn how to connect simulation code to a fitting workflow, then reach for a more serious modeling framework for real inference.

title: Troubleshooting Checklist
text:
Use this first
When code fails, do not rewrite everything. Work down this checklist.

Read the first error message, not only the last line of output.
Identify the object, function, file, or package named in the error.
Run the smallest line of code that reproduces the problem.
Fix one thing, then rerun.
If you ask for help, share the error message and the smallest failing code.



Fast triage








Symptom
Likely cause
First check




could not find function
Package not loaded or not installed
Run library(package) or use package::function()


object not found
Object was not created, name changed, or chunk ran out of order
Run the chunk that creates it; check spelling with ls()


cannot open file or No such file
Wrong working directory or missing data
Run getwd() and file.exists()


Quarto render fails but console code works
A chunk depends on an object created only in the console
Restart R, then render again


Package install fails
Internet, permissions, or system dependency issue
Copy the full install error and ask for help


Model drops rows
Missing values in outcome or predictors
Run sapply(data[vars], \(x) sum(is.na(x)))


glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
Separation or sparse categories
Check cross-tabs; simplify the model


A plot is blank
Missing data, wrong variable, or filtered data has zero rows
Run nrow(data) and summary(variable)





Working directory and files
Start here for file errors.
getwd()
list.files()
file.exists("data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx")
here::here()
here::here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx")
If file.exists("data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx") is FALSE, open SISMID-2026.Rproj and try again. You should be in the folder that contains _quarto.yml.


Packages
If R cannot find a function, ask two questions:

Which package provides the function?
Did I load that package in this R session or this Quarto document?

Examples:
library(readxl)
readxl::read_xlsx("data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx")

library(janitor)
janitor::clean_names(qcrc)
Using package::function() is useful in reports because it makes the source of the function explicit.


Quarto render problems
Rendering starts a clean session. That is good for reproducibility, but it means objects created only in your console do not exist during render.
Before asking for help, try this:

Save the file.
Restart R.
Render again.
If it fails, find the first failing chunk.
Run only that chunk and the setup chunks it depends on.

Common fixes:

Put all library() calls in the first chunk.
Put data-loading code in the document, not only in the console.
Make file paths relative to the project root.
Use eval: false only for unfinished optional chunks, not required analysis.



Data import problems
For the QCRC workbook:
readxl::excel_sheets("data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx")

qcrc <- readxl::read_xlsx(
  "data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx",
  sheet = "Main_Dataset"
)
If a numeric variable behaves like text, convert it deliberately.
to_numeric <- function(x) {
  as.numeric(dplyr::na_if(as.character(x), "."))
}

qcrc$bmi <- to_numeric(qcrc$bmi)
If conversion creates NAs, check the original values.
unique(qcrc$bmi)


Model problems
Start with the smallest model that answers part of your question.
fit1 <- glm(x30d_mortality ~ age, data = qcrc, family = binomial)
fit2 <- glm(x30d_mortality ~ age + bmi, data = qcrc, family = binomial)
If the adjusted model fails, check:

Are the outcome and predictors present?
Are missing values removing most of the data?
Are there categorical levels with very few observations?
Did you accidentally model a character variable that should be a factor or numeric variable?

Useful checks:
table(qcrc$x30d_mortality, useNA = "ifany")
table(qcrc$x30d_mortality, qcrc$intubated, useNA = "ifany")
sapply(qcrc[c("x30d_mortality", "age", "bmi", "intubated")], \(x) sum(is.na(x)))


Asking for help efficiently
Send or show:

the exact error message,
the chunk or line that failed,
what you expected to happen,
what you already checked.

This is enough:
I am rendering Exercise 2. The data-loading chunk fails with:
"Error: path does not exist: data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx"

getwd() returns C:/Users/me/Downloads.
file.exists("data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx") returns FALSE.
That tells us the problem is the project location, not your statistical code.






ReuseCC BY-NC 4.0

title: Model-Reporting Cheat Sheet
text:
Reporting goal
A model result is not finished when R prints output. A report should tell a reader:

what outcome you modeled,
what predictors were included,
what scale the estimate uses,
the estimate and uncertainty,
the plain-language interpretation,
one limitation or assumption that matters.



Common model helpers








Task
Function
Use it for




Inspect the full model output
summary(fit)
Checking coefficients, standard errors, tests, residual summaries


Get coefficients only
coef(fit)
Building a small table or extracting one estimate


Get confidence intervals
confint.default(fit)
Fast Wald-style intervals for lm() or glm() objects


Exponentiate log-scale estimates
exp()
Odds ratios from logistic regression; rate ratios from Poisson regression


Make predictions
predict()
Expected values, probabilities, fitted values, or scenario comparisons


Compare nested models
anova()
Likelihood ratio or analysis-of-variance style comparisons





Linear regression
Example:
fit_lm <- lm(icu_los ~ age + bmi + intubated, data = qcrc)

summary(fit_lm)
coef(fit_lm)
confint.default(fit_lm)
Report on the original outcome scale:
In a linear regression adjusted for BMI and intubation, each 10-year increase in
age was associated with an estimated X-day difference in ICU length of stay
(95% CI: L to U days).
Useful habit: rescale predictors when one unit is too small.
qcrc$age_10 <- qcrc$age / 10
fit_lm <- lm(icu_los ~ age_10 + bmi + intubated, data = qcrc)


Logistic regression
Example:
fit_logit <- glm(
  x30d_mortality ~ age + bmi + intubated,
  data = qcrc,
  family = binomial
)

summary(fit_logit)
coef(fit_logit)
exp(coef(fit_logit))
exp(confint.default(fit_logit))
coef(fit_logit) gives log-odds coefficients. exp(coef(fit_logit)) gives odds ratios.
Report odds ratios carefully:
In an adjusted logistic regression, intubation was associated with X times the
odds of 30-day mortality compared with no intubation (95% CI: L to U), adjusting
for age and BMI.
Do not write “X times the risk” unless you estimated a risk ratio. Odds and risk are different.


Predicted probabilities
Odds ratios are compact, but predicted probabilities are often easier for collaborators to understand.
new_patients <- data.frame(
  age = c(50, 70),
  bmi = c(28, 28),
  intubated = factor(c("No", "Yes"), levels = levels(qcrc$intubated))
)

predict(
  fit_logit,
  newdata = new_patients,
  type = "response"
)
For logistic regression:

type = "link" returns log odds.
type = "response" returns predicted probabilities.



Poisson or count models
Example:
fit_pois <- glm(
  measles_cases ~ MCV1_coverage + MCV2_coverage,
  data = meas_2005,
  family = poisson
)

exp(coef(fit_pois))
exp(confint.default(fit_pois))
Exponentiated Poisson coefficients are rate ratios when the model is specified as a rate model, often with an offset for population or time.
fit_rate <- glm(
  measles_cases ~ MCV1_coverage + offset(log(population)),
  data = meas_2005,
  family = poisson
)
Report the offset if you use one.


Minimum model table
A simple table is enough for Exercise 2.
or_table <- data.frame(
  term = names(coef(fit_logit)),
  odds_ratio = exp(coef(fit_logit)),
  exp(confint.default(fit_logit)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

names(or_table) <- c("term", "estimate", "conf_low", "conf_high")

or_table |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(where(is.numeric), \(x) round(x, 2))
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Adjusted odds ratios for 30-day mortality.")


Interpretation checklist
Before you write the Results paragraph, check:

Is the outcome clear?
Is the reference group clear for categorical predictors?
Are you reporting the right scale: difference, odds ratio, rate ratio, or probability?
Did you include the confidence interval?
Did you say what variables were adjusted for?
Did you avoid causal language unless the design supports it?



Words that usually work
For associational analyses:
was associated with
had higher/lower estimated odds of
after adjusting for
the confidence interval was wide
this estimate is imprecise
For limitations:
This analysis is descriptive and should not be interpreted causally.
Missing data may affect the estimate.
The model does not adjust for all clinically relevant confounders.
Sparse categories make this estimate unstable.


Words to avoid unless justified
Avoid:

“caused”
“proved”
“risk” when you estimated odds
“significant” without saying statistically, clinically, or practically
“no effect” when the interval is wide







ReuseCC BY-NC 4.0

) |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>

    bmi = to_numeric(bmi),
    crp = to_numeric(crp),
    d_dimer = to_numeric(d_dimer)
  )

title: Loops, Functions, and Apply Decision Guide
text:
Main rule
Choose the tool that matches the shape of the repeated task.

If one vectorized expression works, use it.
If you copy and paste code twice, consider a function.
If you need to repeat a task over many inputs, use a loop or an apply-family function.
If each repetition returns a different-sized object, keep a list until the final combining step.



Decision table








Situation
Good tool
Why




One calculation over a full vector
Vectorized R code
Short, fast, readable


Same calculation for many columns or groups
lapply(), sapply(), tapply(), or dplyr::summarise()
Makes the repetition explicit


Same multi-step analysis used more than once
A function
Gives the analysis a name and clear inputs


Need to save one result per iteration
for loop with preallocated output
Easy to debug one step at a time


Need one model per group
split() plus lapply()
Natural list workflow


Need a table or data frame at the end
do.call(rbind, results) or dplyr::bind_rows()
Combines repeated outputs after they are checked


Need side effects such as writing files
for loop
Side effects are clearer in loops





Start with the simplest version
Before writing a loop or lapply(), write the code for one case.
india <- subset(meas, iso3c == "IND")

data.frame(
  country = unique(india$country),
  min = min(india$measles_cases, na.rm = TRUE),
  median = median(india$measles_cases, na.rm = TRUE),
  max = max(india$measles_cases, na.rm = TRUE)
)
Then turn the changing part into an argument.
get_country_stats <- function(df, iso3_code) {
  country_data <- subset(df, iso3c == iso3_code)

  data.frame(
    country = unique(country_data$country),
    min = min(country_data$measles_cases, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(country_data$measles_cases, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = max(country_data$measles_cases, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
}
Then repeat it.
country_codes <- c("IND", "PAK", "BGD", "NPL")

country_results <- lapply(
  country_codes,
  \(code) get_country_stats(meas, code)
)

do.call(rbind, country_results)


When to use a loop
Use a loop when you want every step to be obvious.
country_codes <- c("IND", "PAK", "BGD", "NPL")
country_results <- vector("list", length(country_codes))

for (i in seq_along(country_codes)) {
  country_results[[i]] <- get_country_stats(meas, country_codes[[i]])
}

do.call(rbind, country_results)
Loops are especially useful when:

you are learning or debugging,
each iteration has several steps,
you need progress messages,
you are writing files or plots,
you need to inspect i = 1 before running everything.



When to use lapply()
Use lapply() when you have a vector or list of inputs and want one output per input.
country_results <- lapply(
  country_codes,
  \(code) get_country_stats(meas, code)
)
lapply() always returns a list. That is a feature, not a problem. Lists are good temporary containers for data frames, models, plots, and other objects that do not fit neatly into one vector.


When to use sapply()
Use sapply() when each repetition returns one simple value.
sheet_names <- readxl::excel_sheets("data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx")

sheet_rows <- sapply(
  sheet_names,
  \(sheet) nrow(readxl::read_xlsx("data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx", sheet = sheet))
)
If sapply() returns a shape you did not expect, switch back to lapply() and combine the results yourself.


When to use tapply()
Use tapply() for one summary by one or more grouping variables.
tapply(
  meas$MCV1_coverage,
  meas$region,
  \(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
)
This is a good fit for quick grouped means, medians, counts, or ranges.


When to use split() plus lapply()
Use this pattern when each group needs a multi-step analysis or a model.
meas_regions <- split(meas_2005, meas_2005$region)

region_models <- lapply(
  meas_regions,
  \(d) glm(
    measles_cases ~ MCV1_coverage + MCV2_coverage,
    data = d,
    family = poisson
  )
)

region_coefs <- lapply(region_models, coef)
do.call(rbind, region_coefs)


Function checklist
A useful function has:

a name that says what it returns,
arguments for the parts that change,
no hidden dependence on a global object unless that is deliberate,
a return value you can inspect,
one small example call that proves it works.

Bad sign:
my_function <- function() {
  # uses qcrc, x, y, and model_formula from the global environment
}
Better:
fit_mortality_model <- function(data, formula) {
  glm(formula, data = data, family = binomial)
}

fit_mortality_model(qcrc, x30d_mortality ~ age + bmi)


Debugging repeated code
When repeated code fails:

Run the function or loop body for one input.
Print or inspect that input.
Check the class and missing values.
Fix the one-input version.
Repeat over all inputs only after the one-input version works.

Example:
code <- country_codes[[1]]
one_result <- get_country_stats(meas, code)
one_result


Choosing between loop and apply
Both are normal R. The better choice is the one your future self can read.
Use a loop when clarity and debugging matter most. Use lapply() when the task is already packaged as “apply this function to each input.” In professional work, readability beats cleverness.






ReuseCC BY-NC 4.0

title: Setup Check
text:
Goal
Use this page before class, or at the start of Module 1, to confirm that your laptop can render Quarto documents and read the course data.
Do not spend more than 10 minutes stuck alone. If one check fails, copy the error message, note which step failed, and ask an instructor or neighbor.


What you need installed








Tool
Minimum for this course
How to check




R
4.4.1 or newer
Run R.version.string in the R console


RStudio or Positron
Recent desktop version
Open a .qmd file and find the Render button


Quarto
1.6.0 or newer
In a terminal, run quarto --version


Course files
Current repository ZIP or Git checkout
You can see SISMID-2026.Rproj, data/, modules/, and exercises/





Install course packages
Run this in the R console. It is safe to run again if some packages are already installed.
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "here",
  "janitor",
  "readxl",
  "rio",
  "ggpubr",
  "printr",
  "knitr",
  "boot",
  "deSolve"
))
Optional or take-home modules may use additional packages such as arrow. Install those only if you plan to work through that material.


Open the project
Recommended:

Download or clone the repository.
Unzip it if needed.
Open SISMID-2026.Rproj.
Work from the project root, the folder that contains _quarto.yml.

If you open an individual file without opening the project, relative paths such as data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx may fail.


Run the package check
Paste this into the R console.
course_packages <- c(
  "tidyverse",
  "here",
  "janitor",
  "readxl",
  "rio",
  "ggpubr",
  "printr",
  "knitr",
  "boot",
  "deSolve"
)

missing_packages <- course_packages[
  !vapply(course_packages, requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE)
]

missing_packages
What you want to see:

character(0): all checked packages are installed.
A package name: install that package with install.packages("package_name").



Run the data check
Paste this into the R console from the project root.
data_file <- here::here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx")

file.exists(data_file)

qcrc <- readxl::read_xlsx(data_file, sheet = "Main_Dataset")

dim(qcrc)
names(qcrc)[1:10]
What you want to see:

file.exists(data_file) returns TRUE.
dim(qcrc) returns a two-number result.
names(qcrc)[1:10] prints variable names.

If file.exists(data_file) returns FALSE, you are probably in the wrong working directory or you do not have the course data. Open SISMID-2026.Rproj and try again.


Render a tiny Quarto document
Create a new file named setup-check-local.qmd in the project root. Start it with this YAML header:
---
title: "Local Setup Check"
format: html
---
Then add three R chunks. In RStudio, use Insert > Code Chunk or press Ctrl+Alt+I, then paste the code for each chunk.
Setup chunk:
library(here)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
Data check chunk:
qcrc <- readxl::read_xlsx(
  here::here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx"),
  sheet = "Main_Dataset"
) |>
  janitor::clean_names()

data.frame(
  rows = nrow(qcrc),
  columns = ncol(qcrc)
)
Plot check chunk:
ggplot(qcrc, aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, na.rm = TRUE) +
  labs(x = "Age", y = "Number of patients")
Click Render.
Success means:

an HTML file opens,
the table shows rows and columns,
the age histogram appears.



If the check fails
Use the troubleshooting checklist. The most common fixes are opening the project file, installing a missing package, checking that the data folder exists, and restarting R.

title: Module 5: Functional Programming
text:
Ok, but why?: one-country function

get_country_stats <- function(df, iso3_code){
    
    country_data <- subset(df, iso3c == iso3_code)
    
    # Get the summary statistics for this country
    country_cases <- country_data$measles_cases
    country_quart <- quantile(
        country_cases, na.rm = TRUE, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)
    )
    country_range <- range(country_cases, na.rm = TRUE)
    
    country_name <- unique(country_data$country)
    
    country_summary <- data.frame(
        country = country_name,
        min = country_range[[1]],
        Q1 = country_quart[[1]],
        median = country_quart[[2]],
        Q3 = country_quart[[3]],
        max = country_range[[2]]
    )
    
    return(country_summary)
}

title: Module 5: Functional Programming
section: Ok, but why?: repeat with a loop
text:
Ok, but why?: repeat with a loop

We could write a loop to get stats for many countries.


meas <- readRDS(here::here("data", "measles_final.Rds"))
country_codes <- c("IND", "PAK", "BGD", "NPL")

# Loop setup
out <- vector(mode = "list", length = length(country_codes))
for (i in seq_along(country_codes)) {
    out[[i]] <- get_country_stats(meas, country_codes[[i]]) 
}

title: Module 6: S3 and R Formulas
text:
Some OOP definitions, continued

Method dispatch is the process where you call a generic function, the generic function checks the class of the input, and the generic function calls the method for that class.
A default method is dispatched by a generic function when there is no specific method for the class of the input. Not every generic has a default method.

title: Module 6: S3 and R Formulas
text:
S3 classes (and types): model objects

Many S3 objects you will meet in modeling, including lm and glm objects, are lists with a class attribute.
Some S3 objects are built on other types, like factors and dates, so do not use “is it a list?” as the definition of S3.
Complicated model objects are usually lists where the elements have specific names.

title: Module 6: S3 and R Formulas
text:
S3 classes (and types): help page

Help pages for modeling functions often document those list elements.

?lm

Typing in the code is easier, and reading the output is easier.


formula_cor <- cor.test(
    ~ measles_cases + MCV1_coverage,
    data = subset(meas, iso3c == "PAK")

p-value: 0.00263

# A little cleaning for D Dimer
# First turn the "." into actual NA's and make it a real number
qcrc$d_dimer <- as.numeric(dplyr::na_if(qcrc$`D dimer`, "."))

title: Module 6: S3 and R Formulas

You try it (more advanced): interpretation

A basic interpretation of the coefficient for Remdesivir_or_placebo (coded as 1 for remdesivir and 0 for placebo) might be “Odds of mortality after 30 days in the remdesivir group were 0.48 times the odds in the placebo group, for patients with the same D dimer level and race/ethnicity. The 95% CI for the odds ratio was 0.20 to 1.04. The effect was not significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) significance level, but is suggestive that at least some patients benefitted from remdesivir treatment.”

title: Day 3: ODEs with deSolve
text:
Compartment models: equations
For a simple SIR model:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dt} &= -\beta S I \\
\frac{dI}{dt} &= \beta S I - \gamma I \\
\frac{dR}{dt} &= \gamma I
\end{aligned}
\]
The equations say how quickly people move between compartments.

title: Day 3: ODEs with deSolve
text:
Step 3: derivative function, continued

sir_ode <- function(t, state, parameters) {
  S <- state["S"]
  I <- state["I"]
  R <- state["R"]

  beta <- parameters["beta"]
  gamma <- parameters["gamma"]

  dS <- -beta * S * I
  dI <- beta * S * I - gamma * I
  dR <- gamma * I

  list(c(S = dS, I = dI, R = dR))
}

title: Day 3: ODEs with deSolve

sirs_initial_state <- c(S = 999, I = 1, R = 0)

Plot the result and compare it with the SIR curve.

sirs_parameters <- c(beta = 0.0015, gamma = 0.20, zeta = 0.02)
sirs_initial_state <- c(S = 999, I = 1, R = 0)

title: Day 3: ODEs with deSolve
text:
Practice checkpoint: solution derivative

sirs_ode <- function(t, state, parameters) {
  S <- state["S"]
  I <- state["I"]
  R <- state["R"]

  beta <- parameters["beta"]
  gamma <- parameters["gamma"]
  zeta <- parameters["zeta"]

  dS <- -beta * S * I + zeta * R
  dI <- beta * S * I - gamma * I
  dR <- gamma * I - zeta * R

  list(c(S = dS, I = dI, R = dR))
}

title: Day 3: ODEs with deSolve
text:
Optional stretch: equations
Use these equations:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dt} &= n - b_I I S - b_P P S - mS \\
\frac{dI}{dt} &= b_I I S + b_P P S - gI - mI \\
\frac{dR}{dt} &= gI - mR \\
\frac{dP}{dt} &= qI - cP
\end{aligned}
\]

title: Day 3: ODEs with deSolve
text:
Optional stretch: implementation hints
Hint
Add a fourth state named P. Your derivative function should return four values: dS, dI, dR, and dP.
Solution outline
Start from sirs_ode(), add P <- state["P"], add parameters named n, b_I, b_P, m, g, q, and c, then return:
list(c(S = dS, I = dI, R = dR, P = dP))
Set n = 0 and m = 0 for the first run if you want to focus only on the environmental transmission path.

title: Module 5: Functional Programming
text:
What is functional programming?: functionals

Specifically, we want to be able to compose functions, which you might remember hating in precalculus.

\[g \circ f = g(f(x))\]

In computer science, a functional is a function that accepts a function as an argument.

title: Module 5: Functional Programming
text:
Functional vs. imperative programming: imperative

So far, we’ve been using imperative programming: we save variables, and we update them with new commands. Our code is structured as a list of instructions.

title: Module 5: Functional Programming
text:
Functional vs. imperative programming: functional

In functional programming, we write everything as a function, and we get the results we want by composing many functions.
But we’re just going to show you some useful parts of functional programming that you can include in your regular code.

title: Module 5: Functional Programming
text:
Summary: next steps

Side note: purrr is a modern version of *apply() with a more consistent interface. It’s worth learning and covered in the Advanced R book, but most people still use *apply().
This book also covers more standard FP tools like reduction and filtering that we didn’t have time to talk about.
